input.headline: Why nonalcoholic spirits go from strength to strength

raw.headline: Why nonalcoholic spirits go from strength to strength

winner: My nonalcoholic spirit is currently going from strength to strength, which is impressive because it has absolutely no strength at all.

candidates.text: Why did the nonalcoholic spirit refuse to play cards with anyone? Because they always went from strength to strength—and left the deck behind!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Clever wordplay on 'strength to strength' and the pun on 'spirit' being both a drink and a ghostly entity. Brevity and surprise are well executed."}

candidates.text: Why do nonalcoholic spirits go from strength to strength? Because they’re always sober enough to know when to hold ’em.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'sober' and 'hold ’em' from poker, concise and surprising with good specificity to the topic."}

candidates.text: They say non-alcoholic spirits are rising like a phoenix from the ashes, but honestly, I'm just glad they're not rising *in* me like whiskey used to.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on the phoenix metaphor, specific to nonalcoholic spirits, and naturally funny with a relatable comparison to whiskey's effects."}

candidates.text: I tried to start a business selling nonalcoholic spirits, but it’s hard to make it go from strength to strength when the product is so sober.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'sober' and 'strength to strength' are clever and specific, with a natural flow and surprising twist."}

candidates.text: Why are nonalcoholic spirits going from strength to strength? Because they're finally getting a little more backbone — and no one's drinking anything else!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, uses wordplay on 'backbone' (both literal and metaphorical), and ties back to the headline with a surprising twist."}

candidates.text: My nonalcoholic spirit is currently going from strength to strength, which is impressive because it has absolutely no strength at all.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 9.0, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and specific. It cleverly plays on the double meaning of 'strength' (both physical power and quality) while maintaining natural English and a high level of funniness."}

candidates.text: The bartender said, "I can't serve you any alcohol tonight, sir." I asked, "Why's that?" He replied, "Well, I've got this new nonalcoholic whiskey that'll put hair on your chest and make you forget your own name. But it's still in the bottle.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke has some humor with the unexpected twist about the nonalcoholic whiskey's effects, but it's slightly generic and lacks sharp specificity. The setup is clear, but the punchline could be more surprising."}

candidates.text: The nonalcoholic spirits are becoming so strong that they’ve started bench-pressing the bar counter.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "Puns on 'strength' and 'bench-pressing' are clever and specific, with a surprising twist that fits the headline well."}

candidates.text: Why nonalcoholic spirits go from strength to strength — because they're finally allowed to join the gym.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the connection between nonalcoholic spirits joining a gym and their 'strength' is a bit weak and relies on a stretch. It's not offensive and is concise, but lacks stronger punch."}
